stem: A 78-year-old male with acute on chronic kidney disease is exhibiting signs of severe hyperkalemia and respiratory distress. His condition is rapidly deteriorating with fluid overload and cardiac instability. Prioritize the five most important nursing actions to implement immediately.

itemContext.sbar: Situation: Robert Henderson, 78-year-old male, admitted at 08:00 for AKI on CKD stage 3. Background: History of hypertension and diabetes mellitus. Assessment: At 09:00, patient reported increased shortness of breath and weakness. At 10:00, new onset edema in lower extremities was noted; urine output decreased to 30 mL in the last 4 hours. At 11:00, vital signs: BP 170/98 mmHg, HR 110 bpm, RR 28 and labored, SpO2 90% on 2L NC. Potassium level from AM lab was 6.8 mEq/L. Recommendation: New orders received at 11:15 include furosemide 40 mg IV push (administered). Patient is oriented to person only. Lung sounds reveal diffuse crackles bilaterally. Concern for pulmonary edema and worsening hyperkalemia. Family at bedside needs updates. Any questions?

Recommendation: New orders received at 11:15 include furosemide 40 mg IV push (administered). Patient is oriented to person only. Lung sounds reveal diffuse crackles bilaterally. Concern for pulmonary edema and worsening hyperkalemia. Family at bedside needs updates. Any questions?

options.hoverRationale: This medication stabilizes the cardiac membrane, protecting the heart from life-threatening arrhythmias caused by severe hyperkalemia. It is the first priority to prevent cardiac arrest.

options.text: Increase oxygen to 4L via nasal cannula.
options.hoverRationale: The patient's hypoxemia and labored breathing indicate respiratory distress from pulmonary edema. Increasing oxygen delivery is critical to improve tissue oxygenation and reduce the work of breathing.
The patient is in metabolic acidosis (pH 7.28), which worsens hyperkalemia. Sodium bicarbonate helps correct the acidosis and causes a temporary shift of potassium into the intracellular space.
Insulin facilitates the uptake of glucose and potassium into the cells, effectively lowering the serum potassium level. This is a key intervention to treat the underlying hyperkalemia.
Continuous cardiac monitoring is essential to detect arrhythmias caused by hyperkalemia and to evaluate the effectiveness of interventions. It provides real-time data on the patient's cardiac status.
This medication removes potassium from the body via the gastrointestinal tract, but its onset of action is slow. It is not appropriate for treating acute, life-threatening hyperkalemia.

While an ECG is a valuable diagnostic tool, immediate life-saving treatment to stabilize the myocardium with calcium gluconate should not be delayed. Treatment takes precedence over diagnostics in this emergency.
The provider has already been notified, as evidenced by the new stat medication orders. The nurse's priority is to implement the existing life-saving orders, not to delay care.

topicTags: Renal: AKI vs CKD & Electrolyte Emergencies

rationale.correct: The priority order addresses the most immediate life threats first. 1) Calcium gluconate is paramount to stabilize the cardiac membrane and prevent fatal arrhythmia from hyperkalemia. 2) Increasing oxygen addresses the critical hypoxemia and respiratory distress. 3) Sodium bicarbonate begins to correct the severe acidosis and helps shift potassium intracellularly. 4) Insulin provides a more definitive, though still temporary, reduction in serum potassium. 5) Continuous cardiac monitoring is initiated to vigilantly assess for arrhythmias and the response to treatment.
clinicalPearls: Hyperkalemia can cause life-threatening arrhythmias; always prioritize cardiac stabilization with calcium gluconate.
Pulmonary edema in renal failure can develop rapidly; monitor respiratory status closely and be prepared to escalate respiratory support.
AKI superimposed on CKD presents unique management challenges due to reduced renal reserve and impaired compensatory mechanisms.
The effects of hyperkalemia can be temporarily mitigated with calcium gluconate, but definitive treatment with insulin, bicarbonate, and/or Kayexalate is required to lower total body potassium.

questionTrap.trap: Many students select interventions that address the potassium level (e.g., Kayexalate, insulin) but fail to prioritize immediate cardiac stabilization using calcium gluconate. Others focus solely on respiratory distress without addressing the underlying hyperkalemia and acidosis.
questionTrap.howToOvercome: Focus on the ABCs (Airway, Breathing, Circulation) but recognize that in electrolyte emergencies, circulation includes cardiac stabilization. Prioritize interventions that address the most immediate threat to the patient's life, which in this case is cardiac arrest due to hyperkalemia.

mnemonic.title: Hyperkalemia Treatment: C BIG K DI
mnemonic.expansion: Calcium gluconate (stabilizes membrane), Bicarbonate/Beta-agonists (shift K+), Insulin & Glucose (shift K+), Kayexalate (removes K+), Diuretics/Dialysis (removes K+).
